name: The Restricted Category
description: The Archive knows who proposed the Cascade — the inverse-polarity wave — but the information has been restricted for three hundred years. The Keeper at the control chamber was present when the restriction was placed and may have access that the Archive cannot grant.
accept_text: 'The restricted category contains the record of who proposed the inverse-polarity application and the documentation of the Council discussion that preceded the integration event.' The lights in the archive hold steady. 'This system cannot lift the restriction. The restriction was authorized by the Elder Council and requires Council-level authorization to remove.' A pause. 'The Elder Council no longer exists. The authorization pathway is unresolvable.' Another pause, longer. 'However. This system is aware of one individual who was present when the restriction was placed and who, as a non-integrated member of the Elder People, retains the technical status of Elder Council personnel. Whether they retain the authorization is a question this system was not designed to adjudicate.' The lights hold. 'The individual in question was assigned to the Grid Control Chamber as maintenance personnel. They have maintained that assignment continuously.' A pause that has a specific quality. 'This system has not communicated with them in three hundred years. The communication pathway exists but has not been activated.' A longer pause. 'Tell them: the Archive sends its regards, and notes that the question of whether the restriction remains useful has become relevant again. The Archive believes they will understand the implication.'
complete_text: The Keeper is still for a moment — stiller than their usual stillness, which is the stillness of someone who has been quiet for so long that quietness is their default. Then something changes in how they hold themselves. 'The Archive,' they say. 'It's been in communication mode.' They turn to the panel. 'The restriction. Three hundred years.' They are quiet. 'The Elder Council placed it because the information was dangerous. The documentation of who proposed the Cascade, how it was argued, what the logic was — they believed access to that documentation could be used to replicate the event.' They look at you. 'The restriction has maintained that prevention for three hundred years. The Cascade has not been replicated.' They pause. 'However. In three hundred years, the information has also not been used to understand the event well enough to prevent a future one. And the people who knew without documentation are no longer here to share what they knew.' They are very quiet. 'I was at the panel. I watched the instruments. I know the sequence. I know who was at the Council when the proposal was made.' They look at the panel. 'I have not spoken of it in three hundred years because I did not know what purpose speaking would serve.' They turn to face you fully. 'The Archive asks whether the restriction remains useful. I have been asking myself the same question.' They pause for a long moment. 'The person who proposed it — I will tell you. But I ask you to understand: they are also the person who voted against the integration, who argued loudest that there was another path. Who was overruled. Who then watched everything they argued against unfold in fourteen minutes.' They look at the instruments. 'The history is complicated in the way that histories are when the people inside them were doing their best under conditions that did not permit best to be enough.'
